title: Clinical judgment on exam day: turning cues into safe decisions
excerpt: How to read stems for stability vs urgency, delegate safely, and avoid tempting-but-wrong shortcuts on high-stakes items.
bodyHtml:
Exam items reward a repeatable pattern: identify the most urgent patient problem, confirm whether the stem is stable or unstable, then pick the intervention that matches scope and orders. The CAT Exam may stage findings across tabs—reassess after each new datum.

Stability first

When vitals or trends suggest instability, your first job is to stay with the patient—assess, intervene within scope, and escalate when needed. Avoid answers that delay assessment when the picture is changing.

Delegation and communication

Choose the answer that protects safety: clear handoffs, closed-loop communication, and tasks matched to competency—not whoever is available first.

Patient education after stability

When the scenario is stable, Patient Education, discharge readiness, and clear return precautions move up the priority list—still after any acute threat is addressed.

Pair this mindset with timed Practice Questions, Flashcards, and Lessons so judgment feels familiar on test day.

title: Pharmacology study that sticks (without memorization chaos)
excerpt: Organize drug classes by mechanism and monitoring, then anchor with a few high-yield prototypes instead of thousands of isolated facts.
bodyHtml:
Effective Pharmacology prep groups drugs by mechanism, predictable adverse effects, and what you must monitor before and after administration.

Three questions per drug

  • What is the intended effect—and the main adverse effects?
  • What labs or vitals do I recheck?
  • What Patient Education keeps the patient safe at home?

Precautions and interactions

Watch for bleeding risk with anticoagulants, additive sedation with opioids plus benzodiazepines, QT concerns with multiple contributors, and renal clearance issues in acute illness—verify allergies, baseline vitals, and pertinent labs before giving high-alert medications.

Use short Practice Questions with rationales so you practice retrieval, not passive reading.

title: Lab trends that matter in acute kidney injury
excerpt: Creatinine, urine output, and electrolyte shifts—how to interpret patterns quickly on the floor and on the exam.
bodyHtml:
AKI is often a pattern recognition problem: rising creatinine with or without oliguria, contextualized by meds, perfusion, and obstruction risk. Pathophysiology buckets include prerenal hypoperfusion, intrinsic parenchymal injury, and postrenal obstruction.

Quick checks

Compare baseline to current, watch potassium and acid–base status, and tie interventions to the scenario—prioritize Nursing Priorities such as accurate I/O, medication reconciliation to avoid nephrotoxins, and escalation for hyperkalemia with ECG changes or respiratory compromise from fluid overload.

Red flags

Sudden anuria with discomfort may signal obstruction; peaked T waves or widening QRS with rising potassium demand emergent protocol care.

On Practice Tests, choose assessments and stabilization before lengthy teaching when life threats are present.
